## Distributions {#sec-distributions}

Probability distributions are the most informationally complete way to
represent uncertainty about a quantity. Rather than summarising the range of
possible outcomes through a single number, an interval, or a set of shaded
bands, a distributional display represents the full probability assigned to
every possible value of the uncertain quantity — showing not just the range but
the shape, the central tendency, the skewness, and the weight of the tails.
As @efsa2019 define them, distributions are graphical representations of
"probabilities for different values of an uncertain quantity that has a single
true value," most commonly presented as a Probability Density Function (PDF),
a Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), or a Complementary Cumulative
Distribution Function (CCDF). Additional visual formats include density strips
— narrow rectangles in which colour intensity encodes probability density —
and violin plots, which combine a mirrored density display with summary
statistics. As @vanderbles2019 note, "density strips are the most accurate
representation of the underlying probability distribution around the point
estimate," making them valuable when fidelity to the full distributional shape
is a priority.

The distinguishing feature of all these formats is that they communicate the
*shape* of uncertainty, not just its extent. Two forecasts might share the
same 90% prediction interval yet have very different distributional shapes —
one symmetrical, one heavily right-skewed; one unimodal, one bimodal. Only a
distributional display can convey this distinction, and only a distributional
display allows the audience to read off any quantile of interest without
additional calculation. This completeness makes distributional formats the
most powerful tool in the uncertainty communicator's repertoire — and the most
demanding to use responsibly.

### Practical Example

Distributional displays have been applied in several high-stakes scientific
and policy contexts. In climate science, the UK Climate Projections use paired
distributions — a probability density function above a cumulative distribution
function, sharing the same horizontal axis — to represent forecast uncertainty
for change in maximum temperature over a specific region and time horizon
[@spiegelhalter2011]. This pairing of PDF and CDF allows the viewer to read
both the relative likelihood of different outcomes (from the PDF) and the
cumulative probability up to any threshold (from the CDF) from a single
composite figure.

In food safety and agriculture, the European Food Safety Authority uses PDF
graphs to communicate uncertainty derived from expert knowledge elicitation,
such as the distribution "quantifying uncertainty about how many dairy cows
out of a hundred on average are pregnant when slaughtered in the EU"
[@efsa2019]. These applications are notable because they involve genuine
epistemic uncertainty — the product of imperfect data and expert judgement
— rather than population variability, and the distributional format is used
precisely to represent the full range of what is not known.

In experimental risk communication research, @edwards2012 tested the ability
of lay and expert users to extract probability information from five graphical
formats — PDF, CDF, CCDF, a modified CDF, and a three-dimensional PDF — using
hypothetical scenarios including *E. coli* outbreaks, radiation treatments,
airport screening, and flood depth predictions. The results of these
experiments provide some of the most direct evidence available on which
distributional formats best serve which communication tasks, and are drawn
on extensively in the good practice recommendations below.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Paired PDF and CDF for Climate Uncertainty**

Suggested figure: Figure 5F in @spiegelhalter2011, showing a probability
distribution aligned directly above a cumulative distribution for temperature
changes in a specific UK region, with the mean explicitly marked on both
curves and the same horizontal scale used throughout.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @spiegelhalter2011 or from
the UK Climate Projections archive.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — EFSA PDF for Agricultural Uncertainty**

Suggested figure: Figure 3 in @efsa2019, an EFSA-designed PDF graph
explicitly annotating the median estimate, the 50% certainty interval, and
the 98% certainty interval for the distribution of pregnant dairy cows
slaughtered in the EU.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @efsa2019.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Catalogue of Distributional Display Formats**

Suggested figure: Figure 1 in @edwards2012, which provides a visual catalogue
illustrating ten distinct formats for displaying uncertainty including the PDF,
CDF, CCDF, modified CDF, and 3-D PDF, allowing direct comparison of the
visual properties of each format.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @edwards2012.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Density Strips and Violin Plots**

Suggested figure: Figure 5 in @vanderbles2019, comparing simplified density
visualisations — density strips and violin plots — against simpler displays
such as error bars, providing a visual reference for how full distributional
information can be encoded in compact graphical forms.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from @vanderbles2019.
:::

### Strengths and Limitations

**Strengths.** The primary strength of distributional displays is their
informational completeness. Unlike intervals, which communicate only the
extent of uncertainty, distributions provide exhaustive detail about its
shape — enabling audiences to "read off approximate estimates for quantiles
other than those reported explicitly" and to assess whether the distribution
is symmetric, skewed, bimodal, or heavy-tailed [@efsa2019]. This is
particularly important when the shape of the distribution carries decision-
relevant information that an interval would suppress: for example, when the
risk of an adverse outcome is concentrated in a heavy lower tail rather than
distributed symmetrically around the central estimate.

A second strength is the capacity of distributional displays to correct
motivated or uninformed misinterpretation of simpler formats. When numerical
ranges are presented without distributional context, audiences frequently
infer a uniform distribution — assuming that all values within the interval
are equally probable. A simple density graphic accompanying an uncertainty
range corrects this assumption and decreases the inter-individual variance
in distributional perceptions, producing more consistent and better-calibrated
interpretations across different audience members [@efsa2019].

A third advantage concerns the accuracy of specific probability extraction
tasks. @edwards2012 found that Complementary Cumulative Distribution Functions
— which plot the probability that the outcome exceeds a given value — "led to
the highest probability estimation accuracy" among the formats tested, when
the task required users to estimate the probability that a quantity exceeded
a specified threshold.

**Limitations.** Against these strengths must be set several well-documented
and serious limitations. The most fundamental is cognitive complexity.
"Graphical representations of distributions are difficult to interpret for
non-technical audiences" [@efsa2019], and even technically trained users are
not immune to characteristic errors. The most consistently documented is the
confusion between the mean and the mode: when asked to identify the mean of a
displayed PDF, audiences systematically select the mode — the peak of the
distribution — "unless the mean was explicitly marked" [@efsa2019]. Because
the mean and mode coincide only for symmetric distributions, this error can
produce substantially biased interpretations of skewed uncertainty
distributions.

A second and more serious error concerns the use of CDFs alone. @ibrekk1987
found that when users were presented with a CDF without additional guidance,
some were "severely misled in estimating the mean," frequently choosing the
maximum value of the distribution rather than the central estimate. The CDF's
characteristic rising shape, with its upper asymptote, appears to draw the
eye to the maximum rather than the midpoint.

A third limitation is the confusion of uncertainty with variability. Because
distributions are frequently used in statistics to map population variance,
audiences routinely mistake epistemic uncertainty distributions — which
represent a forecaster's knowledge about a single unknown value — for
descriptions of population heterogeneity [@efsa2019]. This misinterpretation
is both common and consequential, and is difficult to eliminate through
graphical means alone.

Finally, more exotic distributional formats actively harm decision-making.
@edwards2012 found that three-dimensional PDFs — which add a superfluous
third visual dimension to a two-dimensional quantity — "led to poor accuracy"
across all measured outcomes, the worst performance of any format tested.

**Disagreement in the literature.** A direct empirical disagreement exists
between @ibrekk1987 and @edwards2012 regarding the utility of CDFs for
probability estimation. @ibrekk1987 found that CDFs allowed for "very good
estimations of the probability that a value exceeds a threshold," while
@edwards2012 found that "CDFs led to poor accuracy" in similar tasks — a
direct contradiction noted explicitly in @efsa2019. This disagreement is
unresolved in the literature and may reflect differences in the specific tasks
tested, the populations studied, or the visual presentation of the CDFs used
in each experiment. It underscores the importance of empirical testing in
specific communication contexts rather than relying on general recommendations.

### When to Use This Method

Full distributional displays — PDFs, CDFs, and related formats — should be
restricted to technical-level audiences who require precise information about
distributional shape, tail probabilities, or quantiles not explicitly reported
in simpler summaries [@efsa2019; @spiegelhalter2011]. Two-dimensional
distributions, in particular, "should be used only at the technical level, and
then with great care and explanation" [@efsa2019].

The CCDF format should be preferred when the specific communication task
involves helping users estimate the probability that a quantity exceeds a
particular threshold — for example, the probability that a flood exceeds a
specified depth, or that an emission level exceeds a regulatory limit
[@efsa2019].

For non-technical audiences, full distributional displays should be avoided.
Instead, communicators should provide the central estimate alongside a
simplified prediction interval or a box plot, with explicit textual
accompaniment [@efsa2019]. Density strips may provide a useful middle ground
for informed audiences who need more than a simple interval but cannot
interpret a full PDF without guidance [@vanderbles2019].

### Good Practice

- **Pair PDFs and CDFs:** To mitigate the limitations of either format alone,
  plot a CDF directly above a PDF with the same horizontal axis scale, allowing
  users to cross-reference between the two representations [@efsa2019]. This
  pairing is specifically recommended as best practice by @efsa2019 for
  technical-level communications.
- **Explicitly mark the central estimate:** Never require the audience to
  visually estimate the mean or median from the shape of the distribution.
  Always clearly mark the location of the central estimate — and optionally
  the 5th, 25th, 75th, and 95th percentiles — on both curves in a paired
  display [@efsa2019].
- **Accompany with textual explanation:** Distributional displays must not
  stand alone. They should always be accompanied by sufficient textual
  explanation, in the simplest terms possible, that explicitly defines what
  the height and area of the curves represent and what the viewer should
  take away from the graphic [@efsa2019].
- **Clarify uncertainty versus variability:** Because audiences routinely
  misinterpret epistemic uncertainty distributions as descriptions of
  population variability, the accompanying text must explicitly state that
  "the distribution represents uncertainty about the quantity of interest,
  for which there is a single true value" [@efsa2019].
- **Avoid three-dimensional formats:** Three-dimensional PDF displays add
  visual complexity without informational benefit and actively impair
  decision-making accuracy. They should not be used in any communication
  context [@edwards2012].
- **Use density strips for compact density communication:** When the full
  distributional display is too complex for the target audience but simple
  intervals are too sparse, density strips provide an effective intermediate
  format that conveys distributional shape in a compact, visually accessible
  form [@vanderbles2019].
